Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I tried to dedicate time each day to reading through course materials, but with 16 online courses, it was overwhelming. I'd usually sit down with my laptop in the evenings and go through readings and notes, highlighting and rewriting key points since that's how I learn best. But I'll admit, I wasn't as consistent as I wanted to be because my anxiety made it hard to focus sometimes.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling mentally that week. Some weeks I'd be on top of things, studying at set times after dinner. Other weeks, especially when my anxiety spiked, I'd fall behind and then have to cram everything close to deadlines. The stress of juggling so many courses meant I couldn't stick to a regular schedule even when I tried.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing a notification about new content that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because I was always worried about falling behind. The anxiety of not knowing what was happening in my courses pushed me to check the LMS frequently, even if I didn't always do much once I was there.
Q4: Yes, logging in became almost automatic for me, especially in the mornings with my coffee. I'd scroll through announcements and check discussion boards. It became part of my routine because I was constantly anxious about missing something important. The problem was that logging in didn't always mean I was productively engaging—sometimes I was just reassuring myself that I hadn't missed a deadline.
Q5: The written materials and reading resources were most useful to me since I'm a read-write learner. I'd download PDFs and annotate them heavily. The discussion posts were also helpful because reading other students' perspectives helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. I appreciated when instructors posted detailed written explanations rather than just videos.
Q6: I often skipped or minimally watched the video content, which I know sounds bad given how much video was available. Videos just don't work well for how I learn—I retain information better when I can read and reread text at my own pace. I'd sometimes let videos play in the background while I read the transcripts instead, which probably shows in my watch time but not in my actual learning.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I could read through something and take notes that I knew would help me later, I was more motivated. Activities that felt vague or disconnected from assessments were harder to justify when I was already stretched thin across so many courses.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. With my low motivation and high anxiety, I had to prioritize what was required just to stay afloat. When I did engage with optional content, it was usually because I was genuinely curious or desperate to understand something better. Required activities carried this weight of obligation that optional ones didn't have.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download all the readings and PDFs, print some of them out, and study from my notes away from the computer. I have notebooks full of handwritten summaries and concept maps. Sometimes being on the computer made my anxiety worse, so working with physical materials helped me focus better.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes and rewriting key concepts, none of which showed up in the LMS. I'd also study with a friend from another section, quizzing each other over coffee. My best studying often happened when I was away from screens entirely, just me and my highlighted readings spread across my desk.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a reading or resource once to download it, then I'd study from that downloaded file for days or weeks afterward. The LMS would only show that initial access, but I might have read that same document ten times offline. It was just more comfortable for me to work with saved files.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging became really hard, usually when my anxiety was at its worst. Around midterms, I felt paralyzed by how much I had to do across all my courses. The CBT sessions I attended helped somewhat, but there were still days when I'd stare at the LMS and feel completely overwhelmed, unable to start anything.
Q13: The workload was genuinely crushing with 16 courses, and that definitely affected my LMS usage. When I was exhausted or confused about material, I'd sometimes just log in and browse without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities, including some personal health stuff I was dealing with, meant the LMS often got my leftover energy rather than my best focus.
Q14: Many times, honestly. I'd have the LMS open, clicking through pages, but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about other deadlines or just feeling too anxious to concentrate. I'd watch my cursor move across readings without actually processing the words. Those sessions probably looked productive in the logs but were basically empty engagement.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. My anxiety created this weird paralysis where thinking about starting made me more anxious, so I'd avoid it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same. It's frustrating because I knew the pattern but couldn't break it easily.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have these bursts of activity right before due dates, cramming everything in at the last minute. The LMS would show me being very active in the hours before a deadline, then quiet for days. It wasn't healthy, but deadlines were the external pressure I needed to overcome my avoidance.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more with that course, but critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety spike and I'd avoid that course for a while. When feedback was constructive and specific, it helped me focus my studying. But waiting for feedback was stressful, and sometimes I'd check obsessively for grades.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation a lot. Courses with clear weekly modules and explicit expectations were easier for me to follow. When things were vague or disorganized, my anxiety made it hard to figure out what to prioritize. Having 16 courses meant I gravitated toward the ones with the clearest structure and sometimes neglected others.
Q19: Courses that provided written materials alongside videos made engagement much easier for me as a read-write learner. Clear rubrics and checklists helped manage my anxiety because I knew exactly what was expected. What made engagement harder was when deadlines were clustered together or when instructions were buried in multiple places—that overwhelmed me quickly.
Q20: I think that describes me sometimes. I'd be very active in the LMS—logging in, reading discussions, watching my progress—but then struggle to actually complete and submit assignments. For me, it was anxiety-related avoidance. I was engaged with the content mentally but couldn't translate that into finished work because starting assignments felt paralyzing.
Q21: That could definitely reflect someone who studies effectively offline, like I do. If a student downloads everything and studies from printed materials or notes, their LMS activity would look minimal even though they're learning a lot. Performance on exams might be decent because the actual learning happened, just not in a way the system tracks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like login frequency and when you're most active, which is useful. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm anxiously clicking around versus deeply focused on learning. They also miss all offline studying, discussions with peers outside the platform, and the mental effort that doesn't involve clicking anything.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, real engagement often happens when I'm reading quietly and things click into place. It's not about how many times I log in—it's about whether my mind is actually present and working with the content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my mental health struggles and how much they affected my engagement. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes, the anxiety attacks before deadlines, or the relief after CBT sessions that helped me study better. It also wouldn't show that despite my low assignment completion, I was genuinely trying to learn—I just couldn't always produce the evidence of that learning.
